19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Phenomenology & the Brentano School

Lev Vygotsky

1896 – 1934

Inner speech, mediation, and the social genesis of higher mental functions.

A serious reconstruction of Vygotsky’s philosophy of mind has to begin with a warning: Vygotsky did not leave a finished systematic treatise called “philosophy of mind.” What he left is something more difficult and, in some ways, more interesting: a series of works on crisis in psychology, consciousness, higher mental functions, speech and thought, affect, defectology, pedagogy, art, and personality, written under intense intellectual pressure between roughly 1924 and his death in 1934. The philosophical position that emerges is not a simple behaviorism, not Cartesian dualism, not reductive neurophysiology, and not a purely linguistic idealism. The best compact formula is this: Vygotsky’s philosophy of mind is a dialectical, socio-genetic, semiotic, embodied, affective, and dramatic naturalism.

By “naturalism,” I do not mean biological reductionism. Vygotsky explicitly wanted psychology to be a natural science, but he rejected the idea that “natural” means merely biological. The human mind is natural because it is real, causal, historical, embodied, socially formed, and scientifically investigable; it is not “supernatural” in the explanatory sense. But it is also not reducible to nervous tissue considered in isolation. It is a culturally mediated form of life in which brain, body, sign, social relation, word meaning, affect, and practical activity form a dynamic whole. This is why consciousness, for Vygotsky, is not an inner ghost but also not a disposable illusion. It is the historically organized, socially mediated, semantically structured self-relation of a living person.

This reconstruction also has to take theology seriously. Vygotsky’s mature scientific psychology is “psychology without soul” in the old metaphysical sense: it does not explain mental life by positing a separate immaterial soul-substance. But the problem-space once carried by the word “soul”—personhood, suffering, freedom, inwardness, guilt, redemption, prayer, unity, self-relation, and meaning—is not simply discarded. It is transformed. Early Vygotsky’s religious and Jewish writings, especially his work on Hamlet and on Jewish historical consciousness, are not marginal curiosities. They reveal that the later “psychology of consciousness” and “concrete psychology” are partly secular-scientific reworkings of problems that were originally existential, ethical, tragic, and in some cases explicitly religious.

1. Textual and reception cautions

Vygotsky is often received in English through a pedagogical and developmental lens: scaffolding, zone of proximal development, social learning, private speech, and so on. Those themes are real, but they can obscure the deeper metaphysical and psychological project. He was trying to overcome the crisis of psychology by constructing a science capable of studying consciousness without falling into introspectionism, behaviorism, dualism, or crude physiology.

There are also translation and edition problems. Mind in Society is influential, but it is an edited compilation rather than a literal translation of a single Vygotsky text; published descriptions note that material was selected, reordered, and omitted. Similarly, the better rendering of the famous Russian title Myshlenie i rech’ is “Thinking and Speech” rather than simply “Thought and Language,” because Vygotsky is concerned with living processes and activities, not static mental entities.

The non-Anglophone reception matters. Russian materials preserve the philosophical vocabulary of psikhika / psyche, soznanie / consciousness, bessoznatel’noe / unconscious, perezhivanie / lived-through experience, and dusha / soul. Portuguese scholarship, especially Gisele Toassa’s work, stresses that consciousness is not a side topic but a central problem of Vygotsky’s entire psychology. French reception has paid close attention to consciousness, unconsciousness, emotions, drama, and activity. Spanish and Latin American discussions often highlight Vygotsky’s monism, anti-dualism, and non-representational tendencies. Taken together, these receptions help recover a Vygotsky who is much more than an educational theorist.

2. “Soul”: what Vygotsky rejects and what he preserves

Vygotsky begins from the historical fact that psychology literally meant the science or doctrine of the soul. In his Russian Pedagogical Psychology, he explains that the old psychology presupposed a division between body and soul, with the soul understood as a spiritual double or inner substance. The traditional questions were whether the soul is mortal or immortal, how it relates to the body, and whether it is a special spiritual substance. He then contrasts this metaphysical psychology with empirical psychology and notes that criticism from Locke, Hume, and Kant undermined the old concept of soul as a fantasy-product; modern psychology became, in that famous formula, “psychology without soul.”

This is a crucial point. Vygotsky is not merely saying “the soul is unimportant.” He is saying that the soul, understood as an independent metaphysical substance used to explain mental events, cannot function as the scientific object of psychology. Scientific psychology must not begin with a ready-made spiritual entity and then assign consciousness, thought, will, and emotion to it. That would reproduce precisely the explanatory circularity he opposed: mental events are explained by “the soul,” and the soul is known because mental events occur.

But Vygotsky’s rejection of the soul as substance does not entail a flattening of human inwardness. It means that what older traditions called soul must be re-described in a different register. The scientific object is not a ghostly entity but the living, acting, speaking, suffering, desiring, socially formed person. This distinction is decisive for theological interpretation. A Cartesian or substance-dualist theology of the soul will find Vygotsky hostile. A more relational, dramatic, liturgical, or existential theology of personhood may find in Vygotsky not a complete theology, but a powerful psychology of how human interiority is formed through word, social relation, memory, symbol, and practice.

So the right formula is not “Vygotsky has no room for the soul.” It is: Vygotsky has no room for the soul as an independent explanatory substance inside scientific psychology, but he has extensive room for the phenomena historically gathered under the name soul: consciousness, self-relation, will, suffering, meaning, personality, and moral drama.

3. The crisis of psychology: against dualism, behaviorism, and reductionism

Vygotsky’s mature position develops in response to a methodological crisis. On one side were introspective psychologies that treated consciousness as immediately given private experience. On another side were behaviorists and reflexologists who tried to build psychology while ignoring consciousness. On still another side were physiological or reductionist approaches that risked collapsing psychology into biology.

In the French edition and discussion of Vygotsky’s essay on consciousness, the central problem is stated sharply: if psychology limits itself to externally observable behavior and excludes consciousness, it loses what is specifically psychological about human behavior. Vygotsky treats consciousness as a problem of the structure of behavior, not as an immaterial addition to behavior. Human inner thinking influences conduct, and therefore a science of behavior that excludes consciousness cannot explain human action adequately.

In The Historical Meaning of the Crisis in Psychology, Vygotsky argues for a Marxist, dialectical, monistic psychology, but he does not mean that one can mechanically paste Marxism onto any existing psychological theory. His criticism of “Freudo-Marxist” combinations is instructive: one cannot simply remove Freud’s libido theory, keep the rest, and declare the result Marxist. For Vygotsky, a scientific psychology must be rebuilt from its foundations, not decorated with fashionable terminology.

The positive claim is that psychology can be objective without becoming behaviorism and naturalistic without becoming reductive biology. Vygotsky’s mirror analogy is important here: subjective consciousness is not an independent realm floating outside nature; it is the result of objective processes, including the brain’s reflective relation to the rest of nature. Consciousness and brain are part of nature reflecting nature. But the reflective process is not simply a private neural event; in human beings it is organized by language, labor, culture, and social relations.

This produces a distinctive ontological position. Mental life is real, but it is not a second substance. It is a quality, organization, or functional form of complex psychophysiological processes. In his essay on psyche, consciousness, and the unconscious, Vygotsky criticizes the assumption that psychology needs an independent psychic sphere in order to be an independent science. He also rejects the opposite move, in which Pavlovian physiology explains everything while leaving psychology either subjective or redundant. Vygotsky’s answer is a dialectical one: psyche must be studied as part of a complex whole, not detached from physiology and not reduced to it.

The result is a kind of non-reductive monism. Mind is not matter plus an extra spiritual substance. Nor is mind merely brain tissue considered in abstraction. Mind is the historically developed, socially mediated, sign-organized functioning of a living organism. The unit of analysis is not “the soul” and not “the neuron” but the whole psychophysiological and social process.

4. Psyche, consciousness, and the unconscious

Vygotsky’s essay The Psyche, Consciousness, and the Unconscious is one of the most important texts for reconstructing his philosophy of mind. He rejects two mirror-image errors. The first is spiritualism: the psyche is treated as a separate force that acts on the body. The second is crude objectivism: the psyche is denied because it cannot be treated as a physical object in the same way as bodily movement.

His solution is subtle. Mental phenomena should not be treated as isolated psychic acts that mechanically cause bodily events. Rather, the whole psychophysiological process produces effects. The psyche is a differentiated part or quality of that larger process. This lets Vygotsky preserve the reality of mental life while denying that it is a second causal substance.

This also shapes his view of the unconscious. Vygotsky does not accept a Freudian unconscious as a quasi-personal hidden agency that directly causes action from behind the scenes. But he also does not deny unconscious mental life. He argues that it is legitimate to speak of conscious and unconscious processes, provided they are understood as differentiated aspects of the whole psychophysiological process. The unconscious is not a second soul beneath the first soul; it is the potentially conscious, nonverbalized, nonintegrated, or differently organized dimension of mental life.

This is philosophically important. Vygotsky’s unconscious is neither merely physiological nor metaphysically mental in the Freudian sense. It is best understood as psychological material not yet integrated into the system of consciousness through meaning, word, and self-relation. Consciousness is not a spotlight shining on pre-existing private contents; it is an organization of mental life. To become conscious is not simply to “have an inner item.” It is to bring something into a mediated, meaningful, communicable, self-relational system.

That is why Vygotsky attaches special significance to verbalization. The relation between word and consciousness is not accidental. In his discussion of psyche and the unconscious, he notes that the distinction between verbalized and nonverbalized behavior contains an important truth: the word has a special relation to consciousness. This does not mean that consciousness is identical with language, but it does mean that human consciousness, in its higher forms, is reorganized through signs and speech.

5. Consciousness as social self-relation

The central Vygotskian thesis is that human consciousness is social in origin. This does not mean merely that society influences an already private mind. It means that the very forms of higher mental life first exist between people before they exist within the individual.

In The History of the Development of Higher Mental Functions, Vygotsky formulates what is often called the general genetic law of cultural development: every higher mental function appears first on the social plane and only later on the psychological plane. A word or sign first functions for others and between people; only later does it become a means for the child’s own self-regulation. Real social relations stand genetically behind higher mental functions.

He later states the point even more sharply: all cultural development is social; the sign is at first a social means; higher mental functions are internalized social relations; and the psychological nature of the human being is the ensemble of social relations transferred inward and made into functions of personality. This is not a vague sociological slogan. It is an ontological claim about the constitution of mind.

The usual misunderstanding is to imagine that Vygotsky says society “fills” an empty individual. He says something more dialectical. Internalization is not copying. When an interpersonal process becomes intrapersonal, it is transformed. The child does not merely absorb social speech; social speech is restructured into private speech, then inner speech, then systems of meaning, voluntary attention, memory, planning, and self-command. The outside becomes inside only by changing its structure and function.

This has direct consequences for philosophy of mind. Vygotsky is an externalist, but not in the narrow contemporary sense that meanings depend on external reference. He is a developmental and cultural externalist: the very architecture of higher mind is formed through historically given social practices and semiotic tools. The mind is not a sealed interior theater. It is an internalized field of social relations.

This is also the basis for Vygotsky’s striking claim, reported and discussed in French reception, that consciousness is in a certain sense “a social relation to oneself.” Consciousness is not merely awareness of an object. It is the person’s mediated relation to his or her own activity, affect, memory, intention, and word.

6. Signs, psychological tools, and the transformation of mind

Vygotsky’s theory of signs is one of the main bridges between psychology and philosophy of mind. A sign is not merely a label attached to a pre-existing idea. It is a psychological tool: a culturally created means by which human beings organize attention, memory, action, emotion, and thought.

Russian scholarship on Vygotsky’s “instrumental method” emphasizes that psychological tools are artificial devices used for mastering one’s own psychological processes. Technical tools act on external objects; psychological tools act on the psyche and behavior of oneself or others. Later, Vygotsky increasingly uses the language of signs rather than “psychological tools,” but the central idea remains: signs reorganize mental functions.

This is not representationalism in the simple sense. A sign does not merely represent something already mentally complete. It changes the functional system in which it operates. A knot tied as a reminder, a word used to guide action, a counting system, a diagram, a ritual formula, a legal category, a prayer, a scientific concept, or a moral command can all become means by which the person acts upon himself or herself. The sign is, in Vygotsky’s language, a kind of “social organ” imported into the organization of behavior.

This leads to a distinctive view of freedom and self-control. Human beings do not become free by escaping mediation. They become free by acquiring higher forms of mediation. The child who cannot wait learns to use words, rules, gestures, games, promises, symbols, and social expectations to regulate action. Voluntary attention, deliberate memory, planning, inhibition, and conceptual thought are not innate inner powers that simply mature. They are culturally mediated achievements.

Theologically, this is important. If religious practices are taken seriously as sign systems, then liturgy, prayer, confession, fasting, scripture, icon, chant, ritual calendar, and sacred narrative can be analyzed as powerful psychological tools. Such an analysis does not decide whether the religious object is real in a metaphysical sense. But it does explain how religious forms can reorganize attention, affect, memory, guilt, hope, temporality, and selfhood.

7. Thought, word, meaning, and consciousness

Vygotsky’s late masterpiece Thinking and Speech gives the deepest account of the relation between mind and language. The central unit is not the word as sound and not thought as private content, but word meaning. Word meaning is both speech and thought at once: it is the unit of verbal thought.

Vygotsky insists that the relation between thought and word is not fixed but developmental. Word meanings change. The relation of thought to word is a process, not a mechanical association. Thought is not merely expressed in words after being fully formed; thought comes into existence through words.

The theory of inner speech is decisive. Inner speech originates from social speech, but it is not simply external speech spoken silently. It has its own syntax and semantics. It becomes condensed, predicative, saturated with personal sense, and oriented toward the self rather than toward an external listener. Vygotsky describes inner speech as a distinct plane of verbal thought, a thinking in “pure meanings.”

This gives Vygotsky a sophisticated position on ineffability. He does not say that every thought can be directly translated into public words. Quite the opposite: thought and speech do not coincide. Thought is larger, more fluid, and more motivated than the words that express it. In his late notes on the problem of consciousness, Vygotsky writes that meaning is the path from thought to word; it is neither simply the word nor simply the thought. He uses the image of thought as a cloud shedding a shower of words.

The famous conclusion of Thinking and Speech is that verbal thought moves from motive to thought, from thought to inner speech, from inner speech to meanings, and from meanings to external words. Behind every thought stands an affective-volitional tendency: desire, need, interest, emotion, motive. To understand another person’s speech, one must understand not only the words and their meanings, but also the motive that animates them. Vygotsky ends with a striking formula: the meaningful word is a microcosm of human consciousness.

This is one of the most important claims for philosophy of mind. Consciousness is not made of private atoms. It is structured by meanings. But meanings are not cold logical definitions. They are developmental, social, affective, practical, and motivated. A human mind is a system of lived meanings.

8. Meaning, sense, and the affective-volitional basis of mind

Vygotsky distinguishes between relatively stable social meanings and more personal, contextual “sense.” The same word may have a dictionary meaning, but its sense in a person’s life depends on the concrete situation, history, motive, affect, and relation in which it is used. This distinction prevents Vygotsky from reducing consciousness to public language alone.

The affective-volitional dimension is not an add-on. Vygotsky’s late theory says that behind thought lies motive. Thought is not a purely intellectual operation that later receives emotional color. Thinking is rooted in wanting, needing, fearing, hoping, suffering, and acting. This is why any philosophy of mind extracted from Vygotsky must reject the separation of cognition and emotion.

His unfinished work on emotions reinforces this. The manuscript on the theory of emotions was written in the early 1930s and published only later. It repeatedly engages Spinoza and criticizes Cartesian dualism. Later discussions of the text note Vygotsky’s interest in Spinoza’s refusal to split body and soul into unrelated domains, and in the idea that emotion is not merely a sum of bodily sensations but a tendency or orientation of action.

Spanish-language scholarship on Vygotsky’s theory of emotions draws out this point clearly: emotions develop according to mechanisms analogous to other higher mental processes; they are reorganized through imagination, intellect, will, meaning, and social-historical formation. Vygotsky’s Spinozian tendency is not to eliminate emotion but to understand emotion as part of a dynamic system of life, action, and consciousness.

This gives us a strong Vygotskian alternative to both intellectualism and emotionalism. Consciousness is neither pure thought nor pure feeling. It is a system in which affect and intellect are internally related. A thought without motive is psychologically incomplete. An emotion outside meaning is not yet a fully human emotion. Higher consciousness consists in the transformation of affect through signs, meanings, imagination, and social practice.

9. Perezhivanie: lived experience as person-in-situation

The Russian term perezhivanie is difficult to translate. It means lived-through experience, experiencing, or the way a person undergoes and interprets a situation. It is not merely subjective feeling and not merely external circumstance. It is the unity of person and environment as lived.

French discussion of Vygotsky emphasizes that perezhivanie becomes increasingly important in the late work as a unit for analyzing consciousness and development. It is the prism through which the child refracts circumstances. The same objective environment can affect different children differently because each child lives, interprets, and affectively organizes it differently.

This idea is essential for a Vygotskian philosophy of mind because it avoids both determinism and subjectivism. The environment does not mechanically cause development. Nor does the child simply invent meaning from within. Development occurs through the child’s lived relation to the environment. A situation becomes developmentally significant only as it is experienced, interpreted, suffered, appropriated, resisted, or transformed by the person.

Theologically, perezhivanie is one of the most fruitful concepts. It provides a way to think about suffering, repentance, conversion, vocation, despair, consolation, and prayer without reducing them either to external events or to private feelings. A religious event is not merely “what happened” and not merely “what I felt.” It is the lived unity of event, interpretation, affect, symbol, memory, and action.

10. Personhood, drama, and concrete psychology

Vygotsky’s most radical philosophical anthropology appears in his notes on “concrete human psychology.” Here he moves from function to person. The mind is not merely a set of functions—memory, attention, speech, emotion—considered in abstraction. It belongs to a concrete person whose life has conflict, biography, role, action, and drama.

In the Russian notes, Vygotsky says that behind higher functions stand real social relations. He calls the structure of psychological life a “small drama.” The individual-personal is not opposed to the social; rather, it is a higher form of sociality. The psychological nature of the person is social relations transferred inward.

English-language discussion of these notes makes the same point: Vygotsky’s concrete psychology asks “Who is thinking?” Consciousness always has an owner. Mental life is not an anonymous process in a brain; it is the life of a person. The person controls the brain from without by using signs, tools, social means, and cultural forms. Without the whole person, one cannot explain brain and behavior in their human form.

This is where Vygotsky comes closest to a full philosophical anthropology. A person is not a soul-substance hidden behind behavior. A person is a socially formed, self-mediating, historically situated center of action and meaning. The unity of the person is not given in advance; it is achieved, fractured, reorganized, and dramatized through life. Vygotsky’s “drama” is not decorative language. It names the fact that inner life is structured by conflict among roles, motives, voices, obligations, desires, and meanings.

Later Russian scholarship on “height psychology” stresses Vygotsky’s anti-essentialist constructivism: higher functions are constructs; human beings control the brain from without through stimuli; personality is a participant in drama; and verbal thinking itself has a live dramatic quality.

This has major implications. The unit of human psychology is not the isolated mental state but the meaningful act of a person in a situation. Consciousness is not simply “what it is like” in abstraction. It is what it is like for this person, with this history, these words, these conflicts, these social relations, these losses, these hopes, and these possibilities for action.

11. Early religion, Judaism, tragedy, and prayer

Vygotsky’s early writings contain religious and theological material that should not be dismissed. He was born into a Jewish family in Orsha in 1896 and died in Moscow in 1934; his early intellectual world included Jewish history, literature, tragedy, and philosophical questions of meaning.

Chesnokova’s Russian study of Vygotsky’s early Hamlet essay argues that it contains existential and religious motifs: tragedy, loneliness, guilt, becoming, action, inner and outer life, ethical and religious existence, and the meaning of life. She identifies religious plots concerning the relation between the worlds of the dead and the living, sin-punishment-redemption, divine providence and life’s meaning, and the overcoming of separation through restored unity. She also argues for continuity between this early tragic-religious problematic and Vygotsky’s later dramatic psychology.

Vasilyuk’s Russian essay on prayer, silence, and psychotherapy goes further. He interprets Vygotsky’s early Hamlet as concerned with the “second meaning” of tragedy: religiosity, silence, and prayer. For Vasilyuk, Vygotsky’s Hamlet is a philosophy of tragic solitude and prayerful overcoming, where tragedy unfolds on the border of two worlds and unity is restored by prayer. He even suggests that certain passages indicate personal prayer experience, though that stronger biographical claim should be treated cautiously as interpretation rather than established fact.

Vygotsky’s early Jewish-historical writings also matter. Sobkin and Klimova’s analysis of Vygotsky’s journalistic work on Jewish themes notes his interest in consciousness, will, intentionality, public consciousness, and the experience of historical memory. In discussion of Passover and the Haggadah, they emphasize themes of personality, freedom, active creation, will as directed energy, and affective-volitional experiencing of the historical past.

These early texts do not make mature Vygotsky a theologian. But they show that his later categories—drama, consciousness, word, affect, will, lived experience, personality—have a prehistory in tragic and religious reflection. If one reads only the standard educational Vygotsky, this continuity is easy to miss.

A careful theological reading should therefore say three things. First, Vygotsky’s mature science rejects the soul as a metaphysical explanatory entity. Second, his psychology remains intensely concerned with the phenomena of soul: meaning, suffering, will, guilt, inner division, redemption-like transformation, and selfhood. Third, his early religious materials suggest that the later secular-scientific language did not arise from indifference to theological concerns, but from a transformation of them into psychological and cultural-historical terms.

12. Religion as psychological mediation

Although Vygotsky did not develop a full psychology of religion, contemporary Russian work has applied cultural-historical psychology to religious consciousness. Dvoinin notes that Vygotsky himself did not systematically address religion except in limited contexts such as magical thinking, and that Soviet ideological hostility to religion likely limited the development of a Vygotskian psychology of religion.

Dvoinin’s cultural-historical account treats religion as a historically formed system of sign-symbolic means for mastering psyche and behavior. Religious psychological tools include objects, actions and rituals, images, meanings, and concepts. Religious consciousness can be understood as a functional psychological system and a dynamic sense system integrating intellect and affect, formed in social activity and mediated by signs.

This is probably the best way to extend Vygotsky into theology without distorting him. Religious life is not merely belief-content inside a private mind. It is a mediated form of consciousness: ritual action, sacred language, embodied gesture, communal memory, icon, scripture, calendar, music, silence, confession, sacrifice, mourning, thanksgiving, and prayer. These are not accidental expressions of an already complete religious interiority. They are means by which that interiority is formed.

But one must be precise. A Vygotskian psychology of religion does not prove that God is unreal. Nor does it prove that God is real. It explains the psychological and cultural-historical formation of religious consciousness. A theologian may add that the practices mediate real relation to God; a secular psychologist may decline that claim. Vygotsky’s own scientific method does not adjudicate the metaphysical truth of revelation, immortality, or divine presence.

The compatibility question depends on the theology. Vygotsky is incompatible with a theory that explains human action by appealing directly to an independent soul-substance acting as a second causal force beside the body. But he is more compatible with theological anthropologies that understand the soul as the living person in relation: to God, others, language, memory, community, and history. He is also compatible with liturgical and ascetical accounts in which the self is formed by repeated symbolic practices.

13. The extracted Vygotskian philosophy of mind

Here is the reconstructed philosophy in its strongest form.

First, mind is real but non-substantial. Mental life is not an illusion, but it is not a separate substance. It is a qualitative, functional, historically organized aspect of whole psychophysiological-social processes. This avoids both Cartesian dualism and eliminative behaviorism.

Second, consciousness is not a container of private contents. It is an organized relation of the person to the world, to others, and to himself or herself. Consciousness is structured by meaning, sign, affect, and activity.

Third, higher mind is social in origin. Higher mental functions first exist between people before they exist within the person. The intrapsychological is transformed interpsychological relation. The individual is not the opposite of the social; the individual is sociality reorganized into personality.

Fourth, signs are constitutive, not decorative. Language and other symbolic tools do not merely express pre-existing mental contents. They reorganize attention, memory, will, emotion, imagination, and thought. The sign is a means by which human beings act upon themselves.

Fifth, word meaning is the unit of verbal thought. Thought and speech are not identical, but neither are they externally associated. Their unity is word meaning, which develops historically and ontogenetically. A meaningful word is a microcosm of consciousness.

Sixth, inner speech is transformed social speech. Inner speech is not silent external speech. It is condensed, sense-laden, predicative, and self-directed. It is one of the central mechanisms by which social relation becomes self-relation.

Seventh, thinking is affective-volitional. Behind every thought is motive. Cognition and emotion are not two separate faculties that later interact. Human consciousness is an affective-intellectual system.

Eighth, the unconscious is real but not a hidden second subject. The unconscious is not a metaphysical underworld or inner demon. It is the nonverbalized, unintegrated, potentially conscious, or differently organized dimension of the whole psychophysiological process.

Ninth, lived experience is person-in-situation. Perezhivanie names the unity of person and environment as lived. The same external situation may have different developmental meanings depending on how it is experienced, interpreted, and affectively organized by the person.

Tenth, the mind belongs to a concrete person. Functions do not think; persons think. Brains do not speak in isolation; persons speak through socially formed systems of meaning. The full object of psychology is the concrete human being in action, biography, conflict, and drama.

Eleventh, freedom is mediated self-mastery. Human beings become capable of voluntary action not by escaping signs and social forms but by appropriating them. A person controls conduct by using culturally given means: words, rules, images, rituals, concepts, promises, plans, and institutions.

Twelfth, the “soul” is displaced but not trivialized. Vygotsky rejects the soul as scientific substance, but he preserves the seriousness of the soul’s phenomena. The old metaphysical soul becomes, in his scientific vocabulary, the socially formed, meaning-bearing, affective, dramatic, self-mediating person.

14. Vygotsky and major philosophy-of-mind positions

Compared with Cartesian dualism, Vygotsky rejects a separate immaterial substance and any explanation that treats consciousness as an inner entity detached from bodily and social life.

Compared with behaviorism, he insists that consciousness is indispensable. Human behavior cannot be explained by observable movement alone because human action is semantically and motivationally organized.

Compared with reductive physicalism, he refuses to reduce psychology to physiology. Brain processes are necessary, but the human mind is formed through culture, signs, social relations, and activity.

Compared with classical representationalism, he does not treat the mind as a storehouse of internal representations that later receive linguistic labels. Meaning is produced and transformed through activity, speech, social interaction, and development.

Compared with phenomenology, he shares the concern for lived experience, but he insists on genetic explanation: we must ask how forms of experience develop through social and historical mediation.

Compared with pragmatism, he shares the emphasis on action, tool-use, and practice, but he gives a more systematic account of how symbolic mediation reorganizes psychological functions.

Compared with contemporary externalism and extended-mind theories, he is arguably more radical developmentally: the social world is not merely an external aid to an already formed mind; it is the genetic source of higher mind.

Compared with theological anthropology, he is both challenging and useful. He challenges any appeal to the soul as an explanatory ghost. But he offers a rich account of how personhood, conscience, prayer, guilt, hope, memory, and conversion-like transformations can be psychologically mediated through cultural and symbolic forms.

15. The hardest tensions in Vygotsky’s view

The first tension is between early reflexological language and later semantic-dramatic psychology. Early Vygotsky sometimes speaks in the vocabulary of reflexes and behavior structure. Later Vygotsky moves toward meaning, inner speech, affect, consciousness, and personality. The best reading is developmental rather than contradictory: he begins within the scientific idiom of his time but increasingly transforms it from within. Still, one should not pretend that all formulations are equally mature.

The second tension concerns causality. Vygotsky says that the whole psychophysiological process acts, not isolated mental events. This avoids dualism, but it leaves difficult questions about how exactly meanings, signs, and motives are causally efficacious in neural and bodily processes. His answer is systemic and dialectical rather than mechanistic. That is philosophically powerful but not fully formalized.

The third tension concerns the first-person perspective. Vygotsky does not deny immediacy, self-awareness, intentionality, or the relation of consciousness to the “I.” In fact, he argues that a new psychology can study these as functional characteristics rather than treating them as mystical givens. But he does not give a full theory of qualia in the contemporary analytic sense. He reframes the problem: what matters is not raw private feel considered in isolation, but lived meaning in a socially formed person.

The fourth tension concerns theology. Early Vygotsky’s religious and tragic writings are real and significant, but the mature theory is not covert theology. A rigorous reading should avoid both secular flattening and theological over-appropriation. He is neither a simple atheist reductionist nor a hidden religious philosopher. He is a thinker who transformed existential and religious problematics into a cultural-historical science of consciousness.

The fifth tension concerns the status of society. If every higher function is internalized social relation, does the individual disappear? Vygotsky’s answer is no: the individual-personal is a higher form of sociality. But this answer requires a dialectical view of individuality. The self is neither pre-social essence nor mere social imprint. It is the active, internally dramatized reorganization of social relations into personality.

16. Theological synthesis: what a theologian can and cannot take from Vygotsky

A theological reader can take from Vygotsky a powerful account of formation. Human beings become inward through outward forms. The soul, if one uses that word theologically, is not adequately understood as an isolated metaphysical object. It is formed in address, response, memory, sign, practice, conflict, repentance, and communion.

A liturgical theologian can read Vygotsky fruitfully: prayer, chant, icon, scripture, confession, and ritual are not secondary expressions of belief. They are mediating forms that shape consciousness, attention, desire, memory, and self-relation. A pastoral theologian can also use Vygotsky: suffering is not merely an event but a perezhivanie, a lived-through relation to an event; healing involves reorganization of meaning, affect, word, and relation. A moral theologian can use his theory of self-mastery: the will is formed through mediating practices, not merely by spontaneous inner resolve.

But theology cannot simply identify Vygotsky’s “consciousness” with the immortal soul. Vygotsky’s scientific theory gives no doctrine of immortality, no metaphysical account of divine image, and no proof of transcendence. It analyzes the human person as a natural, historical, social, embodied, sign-mediated being. Any theological extension has to be explicit about where it goes beyond Vygotsky.

The best theological appropriation would therefore be analogical rather than identificatory. One might say: Vygotsky shows how the human person becomes capable of inwardness, freedom, memory, and self-transformation through mediated social life. Theology may then interpret these capacities as dimensions of the soul, as participation in divine address, or as formation into communion. But that theological layer is an additional interpretation, not Vygotsky’s own scientific conclusion.

17. Final formulation

The most rigorous concise formulation is this:

For Vygotsky, the mind is not an inner object, not a private substance, and not a mere brain event. It is the living organization of a person’s activity through socially inherited signs, meanings, affects, and relations. Consciousness is the person’s mediated relation to the world and to himself or herself. It is formed from social interaction, reorganized through language and symbols, animated by motive and emotion, embodied in psychophysiological processes, and lived as drama. The unconscious is not a hidden soul but the unintegrated or nonverbalized dimension of that same whole. The “soul,” as metaphysical substance, disappears from scientific psychology; the soul’s existential content returns as concrete personhood, lived experience, meaning, suffering, will, and self-transformation.

That is why Vygotsky remains philosophically important. He gives us a philosophy of mind in which the human being is neither a ghost in a machine nor a machine without inwardness. The human being is a historical, embodied, speaking, suffering, desiring, self-mediating person whose consciousness is made from social relations and yet becomes genuinely personal.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
